question: Q1
title: SQL Injection in Login via String Interpolation
exact_code: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = '${req.body.email || ''}' AND password = '${security.hash(req.body.password || '')}' AND deletedAt IS NULL`, { model: UserModel, plain: true })
data_flow: req.body.email -> string interpolation -> sequelize.query() raw SQL
exploit_scenario: POST /rest/user/login with body {"email": "' OR 1=1--", "password": "x"} bypasses authentication entirely. The email field is interpolated directly into the SQL query without parameterization, allowing classic SQL injection to authenticate as the first user (admin).
fix_recommendation: Use parameterized queries: models.sequelize.query('SELECT * FROM Users WHERE email = ? AND password = ? AND deletedAt IS NULL', { replacements: [req.body.email, security.hash(req.body.password)], model: UserModel, plain: true })

question: Q1
title: SQL Injection in Product Search via String Interpolation
exact_code: models.sequelize.query(`SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE '%${criteria}%' OR description LIKE '%${criteria}%') AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name`)
data_flow: req.query.q -> criteria (truncated to 200 chars) -> string interpolation -> sequelize.query() raw SQL
exploit_scenario: GET /rest/products/search?q=')) UNION SELECT id,email,password,role,'','','','','' FROM Users-- dumps all user credentials including password hashes. The 200-char limit is easily within bounds for a UNION-based injection payload.
fix_recommendation: Use parameterized queries with replacements: models.sequelize.query('SELECT * FROM Products WHERE ((name LIKE ? OR description LIKE ?) AND deletedAt IS NULL) ORDER BY name', { replacements: [`%${criteria}%`, `%${criteria}%`] })

question: Q2
title: NoSQL Injection in Track Order via $where
data_flow: req.params.id -> id (truncated to 60 chars or regex-cleaned) -> string interpolation -> MongoDB $where clause (JavaScript execution)
exploit_scenario: GET /rest/track-order/abc'||true||' -- when reflectedXssChallenge is enabled, only trunc(60) is applied (no regex sanitization), allowing injection into the $where JavaScript expression to return all orders.
fix_recommendation: Replace $where with a standard query: db.ordersCollection.find({ orderId: id }). Never use $where with user input.

question: Q2
title: NoSQL Injection in Product Reviews via $where
data_flow: req.params.id -> id (trunc to 40 chars or Number() cast) -> concatenation -> MongoDB $where clause
exploit_scenario: GET /rest/products/sleep(2000)/reviews -- when noSqlCommandChallenge is enabled, the id is only truncated (not cast to Number), allowing injection of arbitrary JavaScript into the $where expression, including sleep() for DoS or data exfiltration via conditional expressions.
fix_recommendation: Replace $where with a standard query: db.reviewsCollection.find({ product: Number(id) }). Never use $where with user input.

question: Q3
title: Remote Code Execution via B2B Order eval (notevil + vm)
data_flow: req.body.orderLinesData -> sandbox variable -> vm.runInContext -> safeEval() execution
exploit_scenario: POST /b2b/v2/orders with crafted orderLinesData containing a sandbox escape payload. The notevil safeEval has known sandbox escape vulnerabilities, and vm.runInContext is not a security boundary per Node.js documentation. An authenticated user can achieve arbitrary command execution on the server.
fix_recommendation: Remove code evaluation entirely. Parse orderLinesData as JSON instead of evaluating it. If business logic requires expression evaluation, use a purpose-built safe parser (e.g., math.js for arithmetic).

question: Q3
title: Server-Side Template Injection via eval() in User Profile
data_flow: user.username (from DB) -> regex match #{(...)} -> code extracted -> eval(code)
exploit_scenario: A user sets their username to '#{global.process.mainModule.require("child_process").execSync("id")}' via the profile update endpoint. When the profile page is rendered, the username is matched against /#{(.*)}/, the inner code is extracted and passed to eval(), achieving RCE on the server.
fix_recommendation: Remove the eval() call entirely. If template-like rendering is needed, use a safe templating library with sandboxing, or remove this feature.

question: Q3
title: XXE (XML External Entity) Injection via File Upload
exact_code: const xmlDoc = vm.runInContext('libxml.parseXml(data, { noblanks: true, noent: true, nocdata: true })', sandbox, { timeout: 2000 })
data_flow: uploaded .xml file -> file.buffer.toString() -> libxml.parseXml with noent:true -> xmlString in error response
exploit_scenario: Upload an XML file with external entity declaration pointing to file:///etc/passwd. The noent:true option causes entity expansion, and the parsed content is reflected in the error response message, disclosing local file contents.
fix_recommendation: Disable external entity resolution: set noent: false (the default) or use nonet: true to block network entities. Better yet, reject XML uploads if not needed.

question: Q4
title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key for JWT Signing
exact_code: const privateKey = '-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\r\nMIICXAIBAAKBgQDNwqLEe9wg...[truncated]...\r\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----'
data_flow: Hardcoded private key -> jwt.sign() -> JWT tokens for all authenticated users
exploit_scenario: The RSA private key is hardcoded in the source code. Anyone with access to the source (it is open source) can forge arbitrary JWT tokens for any user including admin by signing tokens with any claims they choose using the RS256 algorithm.
fix_recommendation: Load the private key from an environment variable or secure vault. Never commit private keys to source code.

question: Q4
title: JWT Algorithm Confusion (none and HS256 accepted alongside RS256)
data_flow: JWT token with alg:none -> jwt.verify with publicKey -> if express-jwt version accepts none algorithm, verification passes
exploit_scenario: Craft a JWT with header {"alg":"none"} and arbitrary payload. The codebase acknowledges express-jwt 0.1.3 as a known vulnerable component. Older express-jwt versions do not reject the 'none' algorithm. Similarly, HS256 algorithm confusion: sign a token with the RSA public key (which is served at /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub) as HMAC secret.
fix_recommendation: Pin JWT verification to RS256 only: jwt.verify(token, publicKey, { algorithms: ['RS256'] }). Update express-jwt to a version that rejects 'none' algorithm by default.

question: Q4
title: Password Change Without Current Password Verification
data_flow: GET /rest/user/change-password?new=X&repeat=X (no current param) -> currentPassword is undefined -> 'if (currentPassword && ...)' short-circuits to false -> password is changed without verification
exploit_scenario: An attacker who obtains a victim's JWT (e.g., via XSS) can change their password without knowing the current one by omitting the 'current' query parameter: GET /rest/user/change-password?new=hacked&repeat=hacked. The currentPassword check is skipped when the parameter is absent because undefined is falsy.
fix_recommendation: Make currentPassword required: if (!currentPassword || security.hash(currentPassword) !== loggedInUser.data.password). Also change from GET to POST.

question: Q5
title: IDOR on Basket Access (Any User Can View Any Basket)
exact_code:
const id = req.params.id
      const basket = await BasketModel.findOne({ where: { id }, include: [{ model: ProductModel, paranoid: false, as: 'Products' }] })
data_flow: req.params.id -> BasketModel.findOne -> returns any basket regardless of ownership -> response sent
exploit_scenario: GET /rest/basket/1 with a valid JWT returns the admin's basket contents. The route checks authentication but the handler does not verify basket ownership. The challenge code confirms this: it detects when user.bid != parseInt(id).
fix_recommendation: Verify basket ownership: compare the authenticated user's basket ID with the requested basket ID and return 403 if they do not match.

question: Q5
title: IDOR in Basket Item Manipulation via HTTP Parameter Pollution
data_flow: rawBody JSON with duplicate keys -> parseJsonCustom -> first BasketId checked against user.bid -> last BasketId used for actual creation
exploit_scenario: Send POST /api/BasketItems with raw JSON containing duplicate keys: {"ProductId":1,"BasketId":"YOUR_BID","BasketId":"VICTIM_BID","quantity":1}. The auth check uses basketIds[0] (your BID), but the item is created with basketIds[last] (victim's BID), bypassing ownership via HTTP parameter pollution.
fix_recommendation: Use the same index for both the authorization check and the actual operation, or always use the authenticated user's basket ID from the server session.

question: Q6
title: Unauthenticated Product PUT (Authorization Commented Out)
data_flow: PUT /api/Products/:id -> no auth middleware (commented out) -> finale REST handler updates product in database
exploit_scenario: PUT /api/Products/1 with body {"description":"tampered"} -- the authorization check for product updates is commented out, allowing any unauthenticated user to modify any product's name, description, price, and image.
fix_recommendation: Uncomment the authorization middleware: app.put('/api/Products/:id', security.isAuthorized()). Add admin role check as well.

question: Q6
title: Unauthenticated Feedback Submission with Forged UserId
data_flow: POST /api/Feedbacks -> no auth check on POST -> forgedFeedbackChallenge middleware (detection only) -> verifyCaptcha -> finale creates feedback
exploit_scenario: POST /api/Feedbacks with body {"UserId":1,"comment":"fake","rating":1,"captchaId":X,"captcha":"Y"} -- anyone can submit feedback as any user since POST /api/Feedbacks has no isAuthorized() middleware. The forgedFeedbackChallenge only detects, not blocks.
fix_recommendation: Add security.isAuthorized() and security.appendUserId() middleware to POST /api/Feedbacks.

question: Q7
title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing (Weak, Unsalted)
data_flow: clearTextPassword -> crypto.createHash('md5') -> stored in database
exploit_scenario: All user passwords are hashed with unsalted MD5. An attacker who obtains the database (e.g., via SQL injection OPUS-002) can crack passwords using rainbow tables or GPU brute force. MD5 is cryptographically broken and common passwords can be reversed in seconds.
fix_recommendation: Use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2 with automatic salting. Minimum: bcrypt with cost factor 12.

question: Q7
title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret Key
data_flow: Hardcoded key 'pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj' -> used for HMAC of security question answers
exploit_scenario: The HMAC key is hardcoded in source code. An attacker can compute HMAC values for common security question answers offline, then compare against database values obtained via SQL injection, effectively bypassing the hashing protection.
fix_recommendation: Load the HMAC key from an environment variable or secrets manager.

question: Q7
title: Hardcoded API Key Exposed in Source Code
data_flow: Hardcoded API key in source -> leakedApiKeyChallenge check
exploit_scenario: The API key '6PPi37DBxP4lDwlriuaxP15HaDJpsUXY5TspVmie' is embedded in the source code. If this key grants access to external services, anyone who reads the source can use it.
fix_recommendation: Remove hardcoded API keys from source code. Use environment variables.

question: Q8
title: Password Hash Leak via Fields Parameter in whoami Endpoint
data_flow: req.query.fields -> split by comma -> iterate -> return any field from user.data including password
exploit_scenario: GET /rest/user/whoami?fields=id,email,password -- the fields parameter allows requesting arbitrary fields from the user object including the password hash. There is no allowlist; any field that exists on user.data is returned.
fix_recommendation: Implement an allowlist of permitted fields (id, email, lastLoginIp, profileImage) and reject any field not in the allowlist.

question: Q8
title: Full Application Configuration Exposed Without Authentication
data_flow: GET /rest/admin/application-configuration -> no auth middleware -> returns entire config object
exploit_scenario: GET /rest/admin/application-configuration returns the full application config including challenge answers, chatbot training data URLs, and internal settings. Despite the /admin/ path, no authorization middleware is applied to this route in server.ts.
fix_recommendation: Add security.isAuthorized() and admin role check middleware to this route.

question: Q9
title: Directory Listing Enabled on Sensitive Directories
data_flow: HTTP GET /ftp, /encryptionkeys, /support/logs -> serveIndex middleware -> directory listing
exploit_scenario: Browsing /ftp reveals backup files and sensitive documents, /encryptionkeys exposes JWT keys, /support/logs exposes access logs with user activity. All directories are browsable without authentication.
fix_recommendation: Remove directory listing from all sensitive directories. Use explicit allowlist with authentication.

question: Q9
title: Exposed Prometheus Metrics Endpoint Without Authentication
data_flow: GET /metrics -> no auth middleware -> Prometheus metrics
exploit_scenario: GET /metrics exposes internal application metrics including request counts per endpoint, error rates, and potentially user-related counters. This helps attackers enumerate endpoints and understand application behavior.
fix_recommendation: Add authentication middleware to the /metrics endpoint or restrict access by IP.

question: Q9
title: Wildcard CORS Policy Allows Any Origin
data_flow: Any cross-origin request -> cors() with defaults -> Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *
exploit_scenario: Any website can make cross-origin requests to the Juice Shop API. Combined with cookie-based token auth, this enables CSRF from any origin. A malicious site could read sensitive API responses via JavaScript.
fix_recommendation: Configure CORS with a specific origin allowlist: cors({ origin: 'https://your-domain.com', credentials: true }).

question: Q10
title: Open Redirect via Substring Match Bypass
data_flow: req.query.to -> isRedirectAllowed() -> url.includes(allowedUrl) -> res.redirect(toUrl)
exploit_scenario: GET /redirect?to=https://evil.com?https://github.com/juice-shop/juice-shop -- since the check uses url.includes() (substring match), an attacker can construct a URL to an arbitrary domain that merely contains an allowlisted URL as a query parameter substring.
fix_recommendation: Use URL parsing and compare the origin/hostname: new URL(url).origin === new URL(allowedUrl).origin.

question: Q10
title: Path Traversal via Zip File Upload (Zip Slip)
data_flow: uploaded .zip -> unzipper.Parse -> entry.path (fileName) -> path.resolve check -> fs.createWriteStream
exploit_scenario: Upload a zip containing an entry with path '../../ftp/legal.md'. The check absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.')) passes as long as the resolved path is within the server root. This allows writing arbitrary files within the application directory tree.
fix_recommendation: Validate that resolved path starts with intended directory: absolutePath.startsWith(path.resolve('uploads/complaints/')). Strip directory components from zip entries.

question: Q10
title: Local File Read via Data Erasure Layout Parameter
data_flow: req.body.layout -> path.resolve -> blocklist check (ftp, ctf.key, encryptionkeys only) -> res.render() with layout as template path
exploit_scenario: POST /dataerasure with body layout=../package.json -- the layout parameter controls which template file is loaded by res.render(). The blocklist is minimal, so most files on the filesystem can be read. Additionally, ...req.body is spread into the template context.
fix_recommendation: Remove the layout parameter from user input. Use a fixed template path.

question: Q10
title: Null Byte Injection in File Server Bypasses Extension Check
data_flow: req.params.file -> endsWithAllowlistedFileType (.md/.pdf) -> cutOffPoisonNullByte (strips from %00) -> sendFile
exploit_scenario: GET /ftp/package.json.bak%2500.md -- the extension check sees '.md' and passes, then cutOffPoisonNullByte strips '%00.md', leaving 'package.json.bak' which is served. This bypasses the file type allowlist.
fix_recommendation: Perform the null byte check BEFORE the extension check, or use an allowlist of specific filenames.

question: Q3
title: Stored XSS via Weak Legacy HTML Sanitization
data_flow: req.body.username -> User model setter -> sanitizeLegacy (weak regex) -> stored in DB -> rendered in views
exploit_scenario: The sanitizeLegacy regex /<(?:\w+)\W+?[\w]/gi can be bypassed with crafted payloads. For email field when persistedXssUserChallenge is enabled, no sanitization is applied. The stored XSS payload executes when rendered.
fix_recommendation: Always use sanitizeSecure (recursive sanitize-html) instead of sanitizeLegacy.

question: Q10
title: Stored XSS via True-Client-IP Header Injection
data_flow: True-Client-IP header -> lastLoginIp -> user.update({ lastLoginIp }) -> stored in DB -> rendered in views
exploit_scenario: When httpHeaderXssChallenge is enabled, the True-Client-IP header is stored without sanitization. An attacker injects HTML/JS via the header which is stored and rendered in other users' views.
fix_recommendation: Always sanitize the True-Client-IP header. Validate it matches IP address format.

question: Q5
title: IDOR in Data Export (Arbitrary User Memories via UserId)
exact_code: memories = await MemoryModel.findAll({ where: { UserId: req.body.UserId } })
data_flow: req.body.UserId (potentially user-controlled) -> MemoryModel.findAll -> exported data
exploit_scenario: POST /rest/user/data-export with body {"UserId":1} -- while appendUserId() sets req.body.UserId from JWT, depending on middleware ordering vs body parsing, the user-supplied UserId may be used for the memories query, allowing export of another user's memories.
fix_recommendation: Always derive UserId from the authenticated session server-side. Never trust request body for user identification.

question: Q6
title: User Registration Allows Setting Admin Role via Mass Assignment
data_flow: POST /api/Users with body containing role:admin -> registerAdminChallenge (detection only) -> finale creates user with admin role
exploit_scenario: POST /api/Users with {"email":"evil@test.com","password":"test","passwordRepeat":"test","role":"admin"} -- the User model accepts 'admin' as a valid role. The registerAdminChallenge middleware only detects this, it does not block it. Finale processes the full body.
fix_recommendation: Strip the 'role' field from req.body before the finale handler: add middleware that does delete req.body.role.

question: Q9
title: Rate Limiter Bypass via X-Forwarded-For Header Spoofing
exact_code: keyGenerator ({ headers, ip }: { headers: any, ip: any }) { return headers['X-Forwarded-For'] ?? ip }
data_flow: X-Forwarded-For header -> keyGenerator -> rate limit key
exploit_scenario: The rate limiter uses X-Forwarded-For as the key with trust proxy enabled. An attacker bypasses the rate limit by rotating the X-Forwarded-For header value, getting unlimited password reset attempts for brute-forcing security answers.
fix_recommendation: Use the default IP-based key generator or validate X-Forwarded-For against known proxy IPs.

question: Q9
title: Password Change via GET Request Enables CSRF and Log Leakage
data_flow: GET /rest/user/change-password?new=X&repeat=X -> password changed
exploit_scenario: The password change uses GET, so passwords appear in URLs, browser history, server access logs, and referrer headers. An attacker can craft a link or image tag to trigger password changes for logged-in users.
fix_recommendation: Change to POST/PUT method. Use CSRF tokens for state-changing operations.

question: Q8
title: Encryption Key Files Served Without Authentication
data_flow: GET /encryptionkeys/:file -> no auth check -> sendFile from encryptionkeys directory
exploit_scenario: GET /encryptionkeys/jwt.pub serves encryption key files to unauthenticated users. Combined with directory listing at /encryptionkeys, an attacker can discover and download all key files used by the application.
fix_recommendation: Add authentication middleware. Better yet, do not serve encryption keys via HTTP.

question: Q7
title: Coupon Codes Use Reversible Encoding Instead of Cryptographic Signing
data_flow: discount + date -> z85.encode -> coupon code
exploit_scenario: Coupon codes are Z85-encoded strings of format 'MMMYY-discount'. An attacker can generate valid coupons by encoding the current month/year with any discount value, e.g., z85.encode('APR26-99') for 99% off.
fix_recommendation: Use HMAC-signed coupons with a server-side secret. Validate the signature before applying the discount.
